data.title: Is Intel (INTC) Still Attractive After Its 159.5% One Year Share Price Surge?
data.description:
• For investors wondering whether Intel at around US$50.78 is still attractively priced or whether most of the easy gains have already been realized, this article outlines what that price could mean for you.
  • Over the past year, Intel has posted a 159.5% return, with 28.9% year to date, 17.0% over the last 30 days and 23.3% over the past week, which has clearly shifted how the market is treating the stock.
  • These moves sit against a backdrop of ongoing attention on chipmakers as key suppliers to data centers, personal computers and broader technology infrastructure. Recent headlines have focused on the sector's role in supporting AI hardware build outs and capacity expansions, which keeps Intel in the conversation whenever investors reassess large semiconductor names.
  • Right now Intel holds a valuation score of 3 out of 6 on Simply Wall St. The rest of this article will break down how different valuation approaches line up on that score, before finishing with a more holistic way to think about what the stock might be worth.

Intel delivered 159.5% returns over the last year. See how this stacks up to the rest of the Semiconductor industry.

Advertisement

Approach 1: Intel Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow model projects a company’s future cash flows and then discounts them back to today’s dollars to estimate what the whole business might be worth per share.

For Intel, Simply Wall St uses a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity model. The latest twelve months show free cash flow of a loss of about US$11.5b. Analyst and extrapolated projections move this to positive territory over time, with forecast free cash flow of US$4.3b in 2029 and further projected growth through 2035 based on the model’s assumptions. These later years are not analyst estimates; they are extrapolations built into the DCF framework.

Pulling all of those projected cash flows together and discounting them back to today gives an estimated intrinsic value of about US$33.39 per share. Against a share price of roughly US$50.78, this implies the stock is about 52.1% above the DCF estimate, which points to a valuation that is rich on this cash flow view.

Result: OVERVALUED

Our Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) analysis suggests Intel may be overvalued by 52.1%. Discover 62 high quality undervalued stocks or create your own screener to find better value opportunities.

Head to the Valuation section of our Company Report for more details on how we arrive at this Fair Value for Intel.

Approach 2: Intel Price vs Sales

For companies where earnings are less useful, such as when profits are volatile or negative, the P/S ratio can be a practical way to think about valuation because it anchors the share price to revenue, which is often more stable.

Investors usually accept a higher or lower P/S depending on what they expect for future growth and how risky the business appears. Higher expected growth or lower perceived risk can justify a higher “normal” multiple, while slower expected growth or higher risk typically lines up with a lower one.

Intel currently trades on a P/S of 4.82x. That sits below the Semiconductor industry average of 5.83x and also below the peer group average of 9.30x. Simply Wall St’s Fair Ratio for Intel is 6.20x, which is its proprietary estimate of what a reasonable P/S might be given factors such as earnings growth, industry, profit margin, market cap and company specific risks.

This Fair Ratio is more tailored than a simple comparison with peers or the sector because it adjusts for Intel’s own growth profile, risk characteristics and profitability instead of assuming that every chipmaker should trade on the same multiple. On this basis, Intel’s current P/S of 4.82x sits below the Fair Ratio of 6.20x, which indicates that the shares may be undervalued on this metric.

Result: UNDERVALUED

P/S ratios tell one story, but what if the real opportunity lies elsewhere? Start investing in legacies, not executives. Discover our 20 top founder-led companies.

Upgrade Your Decision Making: Choose your Intel Narrative

Earlier this article mentioned that there is an even better way to think about valuation, and on Simply Wall St that idea is captured in Narratives, which let you connect your view of Intel’s story to a set of explicit revenue, margin and discount rate assumptions that flow through to a fair value you can compare with the current share price.

Instead of only relying on a single DCF or P/S number, a Narrative lets you spell out why you think Intel might be a stable grower, a high upside turnaround or a much riskier foundry bet, and then ties that story directly into a full forecast that updates as new data, news or earnings arrive.

On the Simply Wall St Community page, Narratives are an easy, visual tool used by millions of investors, and they help you decide whether to buy, hold or sell by making it clear when your fair value sits well above or below the current Intel price and what would need to change for your view to shift.

For Intel right now, one investor Narrative on the platform puts fair value around US$11.35 while another sits closer to US$79.00, and that wide range shows how different stories about competition, foundry economics, AI demand and government support can all be expressed as numbers rather than vague opinions.

For Intel, however, we will make it really easy for you with previews of two leading Intel Narratives:

On Simply Wall St, Narratives like these sit alongside the DCF and P/S work you have just seen, so you can see exactly how different assumptions about product execution, competition and capital allocation flow into very different fair values for the same US$50.78 share price.

🐂 Intel Bull Case

Fair value: US$79.00 per share

Gap to fair value: around 35.7% below this narrative’s estimate

Revenue growth assumption: 10%

  • Argues that the market is underestimating Intel’s attempt to regain product leadership, with heavy R&D spend, suspended buybacks and a reduced dividend all redirected into CPU and foundry investment.
  • Sees a refreshed CPU roadmap across PCs and data centers, plus foundry, Mobileye and other units, as setting Intel up for higher revenue, a 22% net margin and EPS of US$4.80 by 2027.
  • Highlights execution, funding, sector growth and AI adoption as key risks that could delay or reduce the payoff from this turnaround plan.

🐻 Intel Bear Case

Fair value: US$19.66 per share

Gap to fair value: around 158.3% above this narrative’s estimate

Revenue growth assumption: 3%

  • Focuses on weaker thermal and energy efficiency in Intel CPUs compared with AMD and ARM, and sees this as a headwind in both data center and consumer markets.
  • Argues that Intel’s Arc GPUs and slower process transitions could limit its role in AI related hardware, while competitors continue to build share and pricing power.
  • Builds a case around slower segment growth, modest earnings and a lower P/E multiple, leading to a fair value that sits well below today’s share price.

Taken together, these two Narratives show how different assumptions on execution, competition and capital investment can pull fair value in opposite directions, and why it is important to decide which story lines up best with your own view of Intel’s prospects.

data.title: Is It Too Late To Look At Arm Holdings (ARM) After A 67.9% One Year Rally?
data.description:
• If you are wondering whether Arm Holdings at around US$148.77 is a fair deal or a stretch, you are not alone. That is exactly what this article is going to unpack for you.
  • Over the past week the stock returned 8.6%, with 30.1% over the last 30 days, 29.7% year to date, and 67.9% over the last year. This naturally raises questions about how much of this is already reflected in the price.
  • These moves have come alongside ongoing attention on Arm's role in semiconductors and chip design, as investors weigh how its technology fits into broader trends like AI and data centers. Recent coverage has focused on how expectations around that positioning feed into optimism, but also into debates about what constitutes a reasonable price.
  • Even so, Arm currently scores just 1 out of 6 on our valuation checks. The next sections will walk through the traditional valuation methods investors often look at, before finishing with a framework that can help you build your own, more complete view of value.

Arm Holdings scores just 1/6 on our valuation checks. See what other red flags we found in the full valuation breakdown.

Advertisement

Approach 1: Arm Holdings Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow, or DCF, model estimates what a business could be worth by projecting future cash flows and discounting them back to today using a required rate of return. It is essentially asking what all of Arm Holdings' future cash flows are worth in today's US$.

For Arm, the model used is a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach. The latest twelve month free cash flow is around US$1.16b. Analysts and extrapolated estimates point to projected free cash flow of US$6.38b in the year to March 2031, with a detailed path of annual projections between 2026 and 2035 that Simply Wall St discounts back to today.

Adding up those discounted cash flows gives an estimated intrinsic value of about US$72.44 per share. Against a recent share price of around US$148.77, the model suggests the stock is about 105.4% above this DCF estimate. This indicates that a lot of optimism is already embedded in the price.

Result: OVERVALUED

Head to the Valuation section of our Company Report for more details on how we arrive at this Fair Value for Arm Holdings.

Approach 2: Arm Holdings Price vs Sales

For profitable companies where earnings can be affected by accounting choices or short term factors, the price to sales (P/S) ratio is often used to compare what investors are paying for each dollar of revenue. It is especially common in sectors where margins and reinvestment are important and near term earnings may not tell the whole story.

In general, higher growth and lower risk can support a higher “normal” P/S multiple, while slower growth or higher risk usually point to a lower multiple. That is why it helps to look at a few benchmarks rather than a single number.

Arm currently trades on a P/S of 33.82x. This sits above the semiconductor industry average P/S of 5.83x and the peer average of 9.63x. Simply Wall St also provides a Fair Ratio of 46.80x, which is the P/S level suggested after considering factors such as Arm's earnings growth profile, margins, industry, market cap and risk characteristics.

This Fair Ratio is more tailored than a simple comparison with peers or the industry because it adjusts for company specific qualities instead of assuming all semiconductor stocks deserve the same multiple. With the current P/S below the Fair Ratio, this framework points to Arm appearing undervalued on a P/S basis.

Result: UNDERVALUED

Upgrade Your Decision Making: Choose your Arm Holdings Narrative

Earlier it was mentioned that there is an even better way to understand valuation, so meet Narratives, a simple tool on Simply Wall St's Community page where you give Arm Holdings a story that ties your assumptions about future revenue, earnings and margins to a forecast and a Fair Value. You then compare that Fair Value to today’s price to decide whether the stock looks expensive or cheap for you, with the model updating automatically when new news or earnings land. One investor might build a cautious Arm Narrative around a Fair Value near the lower analyst and community views of about US$39 to US$70 per share, while another builds a more optimistic Narrative closer to the higher end around US$205 to US$217, and both can clearly see how their different stories lead to very different numbers.

For Arm Holdings however, we will make it really easy for you with previews of two leading Arm Holdings Narratives:

Each one takes the same core business and valuation questions you have just seen and pushes them in a different direction, so you can quickly see what has to be true before you would agree with either side.

🐂 Arm Holdings Bull Case

Fair Value: US$205.00

Implied undervaluation vs around US$148.77 today: about 27.4%

Revenue growth assumption: 27.38%

  • Assumes Arm gains a much larger share of data center and edge AI CPU demand, with higher royalty rates from newer architectures and customizable solutions.
  • Builds on the idea that Arm's large software ecosystem and expansion into subsystems and chiplets support high margins and durable high earnings over time.
  • Accepts risks around RISC V competitors, customer vertical integration, higher R&D spending, and geopolitical pressures, but still sees a high premium P/E as justified.

🐻 Arm Holdings Bear Case

Fair Value: US$39.16

Implied overvaluation vs around US$148.77 today: about 73.7%

Revenue growth assumption: 16%

  • Views Arm as already deeply penetrated across major customers, so future growth is tied more to the wider chip market and less to signing new large buyers.
  • Highlights patent expiries in the 2030s, strong competitors, and potential selling pressure from the majority owner SoftBank as key headwinds.
  • Assumes more moderate revenue growth, profit margins that improve but stay closer to historic levels, and a lower future P/E multiple that results in a much lower fair value.

The gap between these two Narratives is wide. That is exactly the point, as it shows you how much Arm's value changes when you plug in different beliefs about growth, margins, competition and ownership. data.title: We Think Valeo's (EPA:FR) Solid Earnings Are Understated
data.description:
Shareholders appeared to be happy with Valeo SE's (EPA:FR) solid earnings report last week. According to our analysis of the report, the strong headline profit numbers are supported by strong earnings fundamentals.

We've found 21 US stocks that are forecast to pay a dividend yield of over 6% next year. See the full list for free.

Advertisement

The Impact Of Unusual Items On Profit

For anyone who wants to understand Valeo's profit beyond the statutory numbers, it's important to note that during the last twelve months statutory profit was reduced by €413m due to unusual items. While deductions due to unusual items are disappointing in the first instance, there is a silver lining. We looked at thousands of listed companies and found that unusual items are very often one-off in nature. And that's hardly a surprise given these line items are considered unusual. If Valeo doesn't see those unusual expenses repeat, then all else being equal we'd expect its profit to increase over the coming year.

That might leave you wondering what analysts are forecasting in terms of future profitability. Luckily, you can click here to see an interactive graph depicting future profitability, based on their estimates.

Our Take On Valeo's Profit Performance

Because unusual items detracted from Valeo's earnings over the last year, you could argue that we can expect an improved result in the current quarter. Based on this observation, we consider it likely that Valeo's statutory profit actually understates its earnings potential! And on top of that, its earnings per share increased by 23% in the last year. At the end of the day, it's essential to consider more than just the factors above, if you want to understand the company properly. So if you'd like to dive deeper into this stock, it's crucial to consider any risks it's facing. data.title: Devon Energy Merger Clearance Raises New Questions On Scale And Risk
data.description:
• Devon Energy (NYSE:DVN) received regulatory clearance as the Hart-Scott-Rodino antitrust waiting period expired on its pending acquisition of Coterra Energy.
  • This development moves the proposed merger closer to completion, with closing currently anticipated in Q2 2026.
  • The transaction would combine two large U.S. oil and gas producers into a single, larger operator with a broader asset base.

Devon Energy focuses on U.S. oil and gas production, and the planned Coterra Energy deal would add scale across key shale regions while keeping the business concentrated in hydrocarbons. For investors, the cleared HSR waiting period matters because it addresses a central regulatory step that can delay or derail large mergers.

From this point, attention is likely to shift to integration planning, capital allocation priorities, and any updated deal terms or closing conditions that Devon chooses to highlight. As more information is released ahead of the anticipated Q2 2026 close, you can reassess how the combined company’s size, asset mix, and capital return framework fit your own risk and income preferences.

Stay updated on the most important news stories for Devon Energy by adding it to your watchlist or portfolio. Alternatively, explore our Community to discover new perspectives on Devon Energy.

📰 Beyond the headline: 2 risks and 3 things going right for Devon Energy that every investor should see.

Regulatory clearance on the Coterra deal takes Devon Energy a step closer to creating a much larger pure play U.S. oil and gas producer. For you as an investor, the key question is whether greater scale and basin diversification outweigh the integration and balance sheet risks that often come with big mergers. Devon already runs a sizeable U.S. shale portfolio with strong free cash flow, and Coterra would add more exposure to core basins where peers such as EOG Resources, Pioneer Natural Resources and Occidental Petroleum also compete. The recent extension and modestly cheaper terms on Devon’s credit facility give the company longer liquidity visibility out to 2031, which may support flexibility as it funds integration costs, potential portfolio reshaping and future capital returns once the transaction closes.

Advertisement

How This Fits Into The Devon Energy Narrative

  • The potential combination with Coterra lines up with the existing focus on scale, operational efficiency and using strong free cash flow to support shareholder returns.
  • A much larger asset base could make it harder to maintain disciplined capital allocation across high decline shale assets, which is a central plank of the current narrative.
  • The extended credit line and specific integration risks from this merger are not fully reflected in the earlier narrative that focused more on organic growth, midstream moves and data-driven efficiency.

Knowing what a company is worth starts with understanding its story. Check out one of the top narratives in the Simply Wall St Community for Devon Energy to help decide what it's worth to you.

The Risks and Rewards Investors Should Consider

  • ⚠️ Integration execution risk if combining two large shale portfolios leads to higher costs or slower capital allocation decisions.
  • ⚠️ Greater size does not remove exposure to commodity price swings and the need for ongoing reinvestment in high decline U.S. basins.
  • 🎁 Larger scale could support better terms with suppliers and midstream partners, supporting Devon’s existing focus on cost control and efficiency.
  • 🎁 A longer dated, slightly cheaper credit line may give Devon more room to time spending on integration, drilling and future shareholder distributions.

What To Watch Going Forward

From here, keep an eye on any updated merger terms, synergy targets and capital return plans that Devon shares as the Coterra deal moves toward the expected Q2 2026 close. How management talks about portfolio optimization, asset sales or shifts in drilling focus will matter for assessing whether the combined company can sustain its free cash flow profile. It is also worth tracking leverage metrics once the transaction closes and how they compare with peers such as EOG, Pioneer and Occidental over time.

data.title: Shareholders Can Be Confident That adesso's (ETR:ADN1) Earnings Are High Quality
data.description:
When companies post strong earnings, the stock generally performs well, just like adesso SE's (ETR:ADN1) stock has recently. We did some digging and found some further encouraging factors that investors will like.

We've found 21 US stocks that are forecast to pay a dividend yield of over 6% next year. See the full list for free.

Advertisement

Zooming In On adesso's Earnings

In high finance, the key ratio used to measure how well a company converts reported profits into free cash flow (FCF) is the accrual ratio (from cashflow). In plain english, this ratio subtracts FCF from net profit, and divides that number by the company's average operating assets over that period. The ratio shows us how much a company's profit exceeds its FCF.

As a result, a negative accrual ratio is a positive for the company, and a positive accrual ratio is a negative. While it's not a problem to have a positive accrual ratio, indicating a certain level of non-cash profits, a high accrual ratio is arguably a bad thing, because it indicates paper profits are not matched by cash flow. Notably, there is some academic evidence that suggests that a high accrual ratio is a bad sign for near-term profits, generally speaking.

For the year to December 2025, adesso had an accrual ratio of -0.10. That indicates that its free cash flow was a fair bit more than its statutory profit. To wit, it produced free cash flow of €43m during the period, dwarfing its reported profit of €18.1m. adesso's free cash flow actually declined over the last year, which is disappointing, like non-biodegradable balloons.

That might leave you wondering what analysts are forecasting in terms of future profitability. Luckily, you can click here to see an interactive graph depicting future profitability, based on their estimates.

Our Take On adesso's Profit Performance

As we discussed above, adesso has perfectly satisfactory free cash flow relative to profit. Because of this, we think adesso's earnings potential is at least as good as it seems, and maybe even better! Furthermore, it has done a great job growing EPS over the last year. At the end of the day, it's essential to consider more than just the factors above, if you want to understand the company properly. data.title: Apple Weighs MacBook Neo Opportunity Against Foldable iPhone Setbacks
data.description:
• Apple has introduced MacBook Neo, a lower priced laptop aimed at the low end PC market.
  • Reports indicate fresh engineering delays in Apple's foldable iPhone project, affecting its development timeline.

For investors tracking NasdaqGS:AAPL, these product moves come with the stock trading at $258.86, with a 43.3% return over the past year and a 102.5% return over five years. The new MacBook Neo could broaden Apple's reach by appealing to first time Mac buyers and users who previously saw Macs as out of budget.

The reported delays in the foldable iPhone effort highlight execution risk in one of Apple's more experimental hardware projects. Together, the push into lower priced PCs and slower progress on foldables create a different mix of expectations for future product cycles that investors may want to watch closely.

Stay updated on the most important news stories for Apple by adding it to your watchlist or portfolio. Alternatively, explore our Community to discover new perspectives on Apple.

📰 Beyond the headline: 0 risks and 2 things going right for Apple that every investor should see.

The MacBook Neo pushes Apple into the low end PC segment where Windows OEMs like HP, Dell and Lenovo have traditionally been strongest. That could open up a large pool of first time Mac buyers and students who previously defaulted to cheaper PCs, while giving Apple more chances to cross sell services such as iCloud, Apple Music and Apple TV+. At the same time, fresh engineering setbacks on the foldable iPhone project suggest Apple is prioritizing reliability over speed for a category where Samsung and Google already have products in market. For you as an investor, the mix of a lower priced Mac and a slower foldable roadmap points to Apple leaning on the existing iPhone and Mac lines, plus services, rather than counting on a near term boost from a new premium form factor.

Advertisement

How This Fits Into The Apple Narrative

  • The MacBook Neo aligns with views that Apple can deepen its ecosystem by broadening the Mac user base and then layering on higher margin services revenue.
  • Delays in the foldable iPhone run against expectations that new hardware categories will quickly refresh the premium iPhone cycle and reinforce Apple’s position against Samsung and Google in cutting edge devices.
  • The specific impact of a larger low end Mac footprint, including any pressure on average selling prices or margins, may not be fully reflected in earlier narratives that focused more on flagship hardware and services.

Knowing what a company is worth starts with understanding its story. Check out one of the top narratives in the Simply Wall St Community for Apple to help decide what it's worth to you.

The Risks and Rewards Investors Should Consider

  • ⚠️ A lower priced Mac could pressure hardware margins if Neo buyers trade down from higher end models rather than expanding the total Mac customer base.
  • ⚠️ Extended foldable iPhone delays may allow rivals such as Samsung and Google to strengthen their position with early adopters, which could make it harder for Apple to differentiate if and when its own device launches.
  • 🎁 A successful Neo rollout could increase Mac unit share from a low base in entry level notebooks, creating more long term users for Apple’s services ecosystem.
  • 🎁 Taking more time on foldable engineering may reduce the risk of reliability issues that can hurt brand perception and lead to warranty or repair costs.

What To Watch Going Forward

From here, it is worth tracking how reviewers and education or enterprise buyers respond to MacBook Neo, how Apple positions it against existing Air and Pro models, and whether management comments on any mix effect between higher and lower priced Macs. On the foldable side, supplier commentary on component orders and any timing hints from Apple events will help you judge how central foldables might be to future product cycles. Together, these trends will shape how much of Apple’s future growth story leans on expanding its user base at the entry level versus introducing new premium device formats.
